risks.label: Second-wave disabling strike on Gulf energy infrastructure
risks.details: The highest-conviction near-term risk because infrastructure has already been hit and the conflict logic now rewards retaliation against energy nodes.
Central banks may tighten financial conditions into a growth slowdown if they interpret the shock as persistent rather than temporary.
risks.label: Policy error from energy-led inflation repricing
Chinese manufacturing margin squeeze spills into trade and growth
risks.details: China's weak demand backdrop makes imported energy inflation more dangerous than in a normal recovery cycle.
If energy support measures expand while yields rise, vulnerable sovereigns could face renewed budget-market tension.
risks.label: European fiscal stress broadens beyond the UK

consensus: The Iran war has become the dominant macro driver because it is transmitting through energy prices faster than through visible demand destruction.
The current phase is inflationary first and recessionary later, which is why sovereign yields and rate expectations are moving so quickly.
Even if futures retreat, physical energy markets remain tight enough to keep volatility elevated.
friction: Analysts disagree on whether the oil shock will fade with policy intervention or evolve into a multi-month structural shortage.
There is active uncertainty over whether central banks will lean through the energy shock or respond more forcefully to protect inflation credibility.
Markets remain divided on whether China can absorb the shock through reserves and policy support or whether margins and exports will weaken materially.

major_developments.headline: Energy infrastructure attacks deepen the Gulf supply shock
major_developments.driver: Physical attacks on Gulf oil and gas infrastructure, combined with constrained Hormuz transit, are turning geopolitical risk into an immediate supply-and-pricing shock.
next_watchpoints: Any verified restoration timeline for damaged Gulf processing and LNG facilities.
Whether military escorts or deconfliction arrangements materially increase Hormuz throughput within 24-72 hours.
Signs of rationing, refinery run cuts, or emergency demand-management measures in Asia and Europe.
major_developments.executive_summary: The Iran war is now transmitting directly into global energy balances. Reuters and AP reporting show damage across multiple Gulf nodes, including Qatar-linked LNG capacity and refinery infrastructure, while Hormuz remains constrained enough to keep physical markets tight even as futures pull back from peaks. This is the kind of shock that lifts inflation expectations faster than it destroys demand, which is why monetary repricing has been so abrupt.
key_facts: Reuters reported Brent crude around $107.07 on March 20 after a pullback, with WTI near $94.84.
Reuters reported that attacks on major Middle East oil and gas sites disrupted facilities across Iran, Qatar, Kuwait, the UAE, Bahrain and Iraq.
Reuters and AP both noted that the Strait of Hormuz normally handles about 20% of global oil and LNG transit.
major_developments.why_it_matters_now: Today's significance comes from the transition from fear premium to actual system impairment: damaged processing, lost LNG capacity, and impaired shipping have made the disruption tangible rather than hypothetical.

major_developments.executive_summary: Reuters reporting shows the ECB, BoE and Fed all confronting a more complicated policy environment after the oil shock. The market message is clear: energy inflation is arriving before growth damage is fully visible, which creates stagflation-style policy tension. That is why yields are rising, growth-sensitive equities are weakening, and the policy floor under risk assets looks less reliable than it did two weeks ago.
next_watchpoints: Flash PMIs for evidence that higher energy prices are already hitting business sentiment and new orders.
Any coordinated policy messaging from the Fed, ECB or BoE designed to prevent inflation expectations from drifting higher.
Further repricing in front-end rates and sovereign yields, especially in Europe and the UK.
major_developments.headline: Global central banks are being dragged into an energy-inflation repricing
major_developments.driver: The energy shock is forcing markets to reassess the inflation path, pushing central banks from easing bias toward conditional tightening bias.
major_developments.why_it_matters_now: What changed today is not just that rates stayed on hold; it is that markets and sell-side forecasts are shifting from cuts to possible hikes across Europe and the UK, while the Fed's easing path is being deferred.
key_facts: Reuters reported that ECB officials kept rates steady but markets are now betting on hikes as inflation risks rise.
Reuters reported the Bank of England held rates at 3.75% on March 19 and warned inflation could reach about 3.5% over the next two quarters.
Reuters reported Fed officials said the Iran war is obscuring the economic and monetary-policy outlook, with markets pushing the first cut further out.

next_watchpoints: Chinese producer-price and industrial-profit data for evidence of margin compression.
Any fiscal measures aimed at supporting household incomes or offsetting energy costs.
Whether Beijing increases diplomatic pressure for de-escalation as economic costs mount.
major_developments.headline: China risks flipping from deflation into margin-crushing imported inflation
major_developments.driver: Higher imported energy costs are hitting China at a moment when weak domestic demand and export pressure leave firms with little pricing power.
major_developments.executive_summary: Reuters highlights a distinctly negative inflation scenario for China: producer costs rise, but weak demand prevents firms from passing them on. That would undermine margins rather than revive nominal growth. In system terms, this matters because China is the world's manufacturing shock absorber; if that absorber weakens while energy stays high, the global disinflation story breaks down further.
key_facts: Reuters reported Brent crude has risen about 45% since late February.
Reuters reported economists fear China could move from deflation toward 'bad inflation' driven by input costs rather than demand.
Reuters reported the conflict also delayed a planned Xi-Trump meeting aimed at easing broader tensions.
major_developments.why_it_matters_now: Today's relevance is the shift in analytical framing: higher oil is no longer just a headline risk for China, but a mechanism that could worsen profits, suppress wages, and weaken both domestic demand and external competitiveness.

major_developments.headline: UK gilt stress is turning the energy shock into a fiscal problem
major_developments.driver: An energy-driven inflation shock is colliding with already fragile public finances, lifting borrowing costs and narrowing the UK's fiscal room to respond.
next_watchpoints: Whether the Treasury signals targeted energy support or fiscal tightening ahead of the next budget cycle.
Further moves in short-dated gilts as markets price additional BoE tightening risk.
Any broadening of similar fiscal stress signals to other energy-importing European sovereigns.
major_developments.executive_summary: Reuters and the FT both show the UK moving into a more brittle phase of the shock. February borrowing overshot expectations, gilt yields have surged, and the Bank of England's hawkish tone raises the odds that inflation protection comes at the cost of weaker fiscal flexibility. This matters beyond Britain because it previews how energy-importing economies with thin policy space may struggle if the Gulf disruption persists.
key_facts: Reuters reported UK public sector net borrowing hit £14.3 billion in February versus expectations of about £8.5 billion.
Reuters reported total borrowing for the fiscal year to date reached £125.9 billion.
The Financial Times reported UK 10-year borrowing costs rose above 5%, the highest since 2008.
major_developments.why_it_matters_now: The UK is becoming a clean example of second-order transmission: not just higher fuel costs, but higher debt service, renewed subsidy pressure, and a weaker budget position all at once.
Ukraine's deployment is an early indicator that the conflict is not only redrawing energy flows but also accelerating a new marketplace for low-cost infrastructure defense expertise.
key_facts: Reuters reported Ukraine deployed more than 200 specialists to the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait and Jordan.
The deployments focus on protecting critical infrastructure from low-cost kamikaze drones.
Reuters reported some Gulf states sought alternatives because traditional air-defense responses are expensive and supply constrained.
major_developments.executive_summary: Reuters reported Ukraine has sent units to five Middle Eastern states to help counter Iranian-style drone threats. The move matters because it shows Gulf states are adapting to the economics of modern air defense: using battle-tested, lower-cost intercept methods where premium Western systems are expensive or supply-constrained. In a prolonged conflict, that could alter defense procurement priorities across the region.
major_developments.headline: Gulf infrastructure defense is being internationalized in real time
major_developments.driver: Repeated drone and missile attacks on Gulf infrastructure are creating demand for new air-defense and counter-drone partners beyond traditional U.S.-supplied systems.
next_watchpoints: Whether additional countries request similar counter-drone support or training.
Any evidence that lower-cost drone interception materially reduces infrastructure damage.
Changes in Gulf defense procurement toward layered, cheaper counter-UAS solutions.

briefings.watchlist: Watch for three things over the next 24-72 hours: verified restoration or further degradation of Gulf energy infrastructure, any operational move to secure Hormuz transit, and whether incoming activity data begins to show the first stagflation-style hit to business sentiment.
strategic_context: This is no longer a classic geopolitical headline cycle. The key strategic shift is that the war has started to impair the physical systems that anchor global inflation, trade costs and industrial confidence. Once that happens, the story moves from diplomacy and battlefield updates into monetary policy, sovereign funding conditions and corporate margin compression.

The second-order implication is that the global economy is being forced into a more brittle operating environment just as many policymakers had been preparing for gradual easing. Energy-importing economies, rate-sensitive sectors and export manufacturers are now exposed to the same shock from different angles, which raises the probability of cross-market stress even if the military conflict itself remains geographically concentrated.

escalating: Attacks on oil and gas assets across the Gulf are broadening the supply threat from shipping risk to physical infrastructure damage.
Central-bank messaging is turning materially more hawkish as policymakers confront the possibility of energy-led inflation persistence.
China's macro risk is worsening because higher import costs are colliding with weak domestic demand and tariff pressure.
new: The market is no longer treating the Iran war as a contained military event; it is now being priced as a broad energy-inflation regime shock.
Ukraine's deployment of counter-drone units into Gulf states shows the conflict is already reshaping external security partnerships around critical infrastructure.
watch: Whether Gulf energy infrastructure suffers a second wave of disabling strikes.
Any operational announcement on escorted transit or reopening capacity through the Strait of Hormuz.
Flash PMIs and inflation-sensitive market pricing next week for evidence the shock is spilling into activity expectations.
Whether the U.S. moves from supply relief discussions to direct action against Iranian export infrastructure.
de_escalating: Oil prices eased from intraday extremes as the U.S. and allies discussed supply relief, reserve releases, and reopening Hormuz routes.
Equity markets remain under pressure but have not yet moved into full disorder, suggesting investors still expect some policy buffering.

macro_indicators.title: Brent Crude
macro_indicators.details: Prices eased on supply-relief discussions, but the level still reflects a persistent war premium and impaired physical flows.

macro_indicators.title: European Gas
macro_indicators.details: Reuters reported a one-day surge after attacks linked to Qatar's gas system intensified LNG supply fears.
The UK is combining inflation anxiety, fiscal strain and tighter-rate expectations into a visible sovereign-stress signal.
macro_indicators.title: UK 10-Year Gilt Yield
U.S. 30-Year Mortgage Rate
macro_indicators.details: Higher Treasury yields tied to the energy shock are already feeding into real-economy financing costs.

today_in_60_seconds.target: Strait of Hormuz and Gulf energy system
today_in_60_seconds.headline: Hormuz disruption keeps global energy shock in control of the tape
today_in_60_seconds.summary: The Iran war has shifted from a regional military story into a live global supply shock, with attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure, constrained Hormuz transit, and central banks rapidly repricing inflation risk.

today_in_60_seconds.headline: Rate-cut bets keep getting pushed out as oil shock hits policy outlook
China faces risk of imported cost shock without demand recovery
UK fiscal fragility meets energy inflation shock
Regional war is pulling in new security actors around Gulf infrastructure

events.details: Brent holds near $107-$109 even after a pullback, while central banks and sovereign bond markets shift toward a higher-for-longer inflation response.
events.headline: Markets reprice prolonged energy disruption

events.details: The Bank of England keeps rates at 3.75% and warns inflation could rise to around 3.5% in coming quarters.
events.headline: BoE hold turns hawkish

events.details: Strikes on major Gulf oil and gas assets deepen the shift from shipping disruption to direct production and processing losses.
events.headline: Energy infrastructure attacks broaden

events.details: The U.S.-Israel attack on Iran starts a conflict cycle that rapidly injects geopolitical risk into oil, gas, rates and freight pricing.
events.headline: War begins to spill into global pricing

timeline_context.title: Iran War to Global Energy Shock
